Israel was defeated by the Philistines. Sin, a cursed thing, was in their camp and gave their enemies every advantage they could want. They recognized God’s hand in their trouble; but instead of submitting, they spoke angrily, unaware of any just reason they had given him. A person's foolishness leads them astray, and then their heart is troubled and finds fault with the Lord. They thought that by bringing the ark into their camp, they could force God to appear for them. Those who have turned away from a life of religion sometimes discover great fondness for outward practices, as if those would save them; as if the ark, God’s throne, in the camp, would bring them to heaven, even though the world and sinful desires are on the throne in their heart.
The taking of the ark was a severe judgment upon Israel and a clear sign of God's displeasure. Let no one think to hide from God's wrath under the guise of outward profession.

The defeat of the army was very painful for Eli as a judge; the news of the death of his two sons, whom he had treated with kindness, and who, as he feared, died without repentance, grieved him as a father; yet there was a greater concern on his spirit. And when the messenger finished his story with, “The ark of God is taken,” he was struck to the heart and died immediately. A person may die painfully, but not eternally; may meet an untimely end, yet have peace.

The wife of Phinehas seems to have been a devout person. Her dying regret was for the loss of the ark and the departure of God’s glory from Israel. What is any earthly joy to her who feels herself dying? No joy but that which is spiritual and divine will be sufficient then; death is too serious a thing to allow for the enjoyment of any earthly pleasure. What is it to one who is lamenting the loss of the ark? What pleasure can we take in our possessions and enjoyments if we lack God’s word and ordinances; especially if we lack the comfort of his gracious presence and the light of his favor? If God goes, glory goes, and all good goes. Woe to us if he departs! But though the glory is withdrawn from one sinful nation, city, or village after another, it will never depart altogether, but shines forth in one place when eclipsed in another.
